narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream despite coming from a lower-middle income family, and we appreciate the strength that brings to your journey. The admissions model evaluated your profile, including your undergraduate GPA of 2.50, LSAT score of 36, first-year school decile of 1st, third-year school decile of 1st, family income bracket, and your plan to attend full-time, which is the typical path for most students. It predicted a 67% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which is why your application was rejected—law schools use this to prioritize applicants likely to succeed and pass the bar promptly. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your third-year school decile ranking in the bottom 10%, well below the average of around the middle of the pack, signaling to the model a notable dip in performance that stood out strongly. Your first-year decile in the bottom 10% also contributed toward failure, though with less force than your later performance. On a brighter note, your LSAT score of 36, close to the dataset average of 36.7, actively pulled the prediction toward passing, showing your potential in that key area. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.50, lower than the typical 3.21, added some push toward failure but wasn't as dominant. Meanwhile, your lower-middle family income and full-time enrollment plans nudged things slightly toward success, aligning with patterns for many who pass. Compared to typical students, your academic deciles are in the lowest tier while your LSAT holds steady, creating a profile the model sees as riskier for bar success than average. This isn't a judgment on your worth or resilience—it's just one data-driven lens. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it further, strengthening your later-year grades through targeted study or additional courses, and exploring programs with strong bar prep support tailored for diverse students like you. You're capable of turning this around, and many in similar situations have succeeded with persistence. Keep pushing; your story is far from over.
